coredata.dc:title: Upconversion carbon quantum dots as visible light responsive component for efficient enhancement of photocatalytic performance
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Ke, Jun
Li, Xinyong
Zhao, Qidong
Liu, Baojun
Liu, Shaomin
Wang, Shaobin
coredata.dc:description: It finds that CQDs synthesized by hydrothermal method possess obvious upconversion properties that could transfer low energy photons to high energy photons, resulting in enhanced visible light response and utilization. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified TiO2 photocatylysts were successfully prepared by a facile sol-gel method. Photophysical and surficial properties of the as-prepared composite photocatalyst were investigated in details. Furthermore, photocatalytic performance was tested by degrading methylene blue (MB) under visible light irradiation. The degradation efficiency of methylene blue (MB) is as high as 90% within 120min, which is 3.6 times higher than that of pure TiO2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Quantum dots
Upconversion
Photocatalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and optical properties of water-soluble carbon QDs
Fabrication and characterization of CQDs-TiO2 photocatalysts

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorption spectra of the CQDs prepared by different reaction temperatures (a) and times (b).
(a) Emission and (b) upconversion photoluminescence of the as-prepared CQDs under different excitation wavelengths.
TEM image (a) and FTIR spectrum (b) of the as-prepared CQDs.
XRD patterns of controlled TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 with different concentrations of CQDs.
SEM images of CQDs-TiO2 photocatalysts (A and B) and pure TiO2 (C and D).
XPS spectra of CQDs/TiO2 composites. (a) Full survey, (b) Ti 2p spectra, (c) C 1s spectra, and (d) O 1s spectra.
UV–Vis absorbance spectra of controlled TiO2 and CQDs-TiO2 with 10mL CQDs solution.
(a) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and (b) pore size distribution plots of CQDs-TiO2 and pure TiO2.
Degradation efficiency (a) and kinetic curves (b) of MB at different amounts of CQDs sensitized TiO2.
Degradation efficiency (a) and kinetic curves (b) of MB over 10mL-CQDs sensitized TiO2 at different pH values.
The EPR spectra of hydroxyl radicals over the CQDs-TiO2 heterojunction system under visible light irradiation.
Proposed mechanism for photocatalytic process over CQDs-TiO2.
Schematic representation of the synthesis process of CQDs-TiO2 microsphere.

head.ce:title: Upconversion carbon quantum dots as visible light responsive component for efficient enhancement of photocatalytic performance

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemical
Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering (MOE)
School of Environmental Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It finds that CQDs synthesized by hydrothermal method possess obvious upconversion properties that could transfer low energy photons to high energy photons, resulting in enhanced visible light response and utilization. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified TiO2 photocatylysts were successfully prepared by a facile sol-gel method. Photophysical and surficial properties of the as-prepared composite photocatalyst were investigated in details. Furthermore, photocatalytic performance was tested by degrading methylene blue (MB) under visible light irradiation. The degradation efficiency of methylene blue (MB) is as high as 90% within 120min, which is 3.6 times higher than that of pure TiO2.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon QDs (CQDs), being a new class of carbon-related materials that are nontoxic, biocompatible, and eco-friendly relative to transition metal semiconductor QDs, possess strong size- and excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence (PL) properties arising from quantum confinement effects [1–3]. Usually, the PL quantum yield of bare CQDs is low due to the existence of many emissive traps on the surface, which is vital drawback in lighting area, such as photoluminescence analysis, light emitting diodes (LED), and bioimaging [4–7]. Therefore, it is necessary to passivate the surface of CQDs to improve the brightness performance [8,9]. Nevertheless, the demonstrated rapid accepting and transferring properties of electrons over carbon nanomaterials provide a high efficiency way to control the flow of photoinduced charge carriers [10–12]. It is well-known that effective harvesting of solar energy and inhibition of charge carrier recombination are two key aspects in semiconductor-based photocatalysis [13]. These properties of carbon QDs, such as high absorption coefficient and fast electron transportation, could be used to achieve the aim of enhancing the photocatalytic performance by constructing the heterojunction system between carbon QDs and semiconductors [14–16]. Based on the strategies, a series of efficient catalysts based on CQDs have been achieved, such as CQDs/TiO2 [17,18], CQDs/SiO2 [19], and CQDs/Fe2O3 [20,21].

On the other hand, the unique PL property of CQDs called by upconversion has been found, which could transfer low energy photon to high energy photon over CQDs by sequential absorbing two or multiple longer wavelength photons [22–24]. In general, the conventional inorganic semiconductor-based QDs absorb short wavelength photons and then emit long wavelength photons. The loss of energy is consumed in the term of thermal dissipation, which is harmful for the efficient utilization of solar energy. The upconversion property of CQDs provides new approach to improve the usage efficiency of QDs in sensing, bioimaging, and photoelectrical conversion (PEC) [25–27]. For example, Lin et al. took advantage of the upconversion properties of CQDs to assemble high-quality, full-color displays, and flexible CQDs-based LED [6]. However, upconversion properties of colloidal CQDs have been seldom reported for expanding sunlight response and enhancing photocatalytic performance of catalysts [28].

Herein, we report the coupling of upconversion CQDs and TiO2 microspheres via a facile sol-gel method, which exhibits improved photocatalytic activity for organic dyes (methylene blue) under visible-light irradiation. We systematically investigated photophysical properties of CQDs prepared by hydrothermal method, in particular upconversion process under low photons irradiation. The as-prepared photocatalysts displayed enhanced visible-light response after coupling CQDs due to the upconversion of photo-generated electrons and holes. Accordingly, a reasonable mechanism was proposed to illustrate the critical roles of CQDs in the photocatalytic process.

l-ascorbic acid were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Titanium(IV)-n-butoxide (Ti(OBu)4), and iminodiacetic acid were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. All other reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

First, 1.5g of l-ascorbic acid was dissolved in deionized water (30mL), and anhydrous ethanol (30mL) was added and stirred vigorously for at least 2h to obtain transparent solution. 25mL of the solution was transferred to Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and maintained at 180°C for 4h, followed by cooled to room temperature. The dark brown product was extracted with dichloromethane, and the water-phase solution was dialyzed to remove impurities for 2days through dialysis membrane (retained molecular weight: 3000Da). Finally, yellow aqueous solution containing CQDs was obtained for further use.

1.8g of Ti(OBu)4 and 4.6g of iminodiacetic acid were dissolved in 35ml of absolute ethanol, respectively. Then, iminodiacetic acid solution was slowly added into the Ti precursor solution. The pH value of the mixed solution was adjusted to 7.5 by using concentrated NaOH solution. After vigorously stirred for 2h, 25mL of absolute ethanol was added into the mixture solution drop by drop. The white precipitations were collected and washed by using deionized water and ethanol at least three times, then dried in vacuum at 60°C for 8h. Finally, the as-obtained products were placed in muffin furnace and annealed at 300°C for 2h in air to get the TiO2 microspheres.

0.2g of the as-prepared TiO2 was dispersed in 50mL of absolute ethanol and sonicated for 30min. And then certain volume of CQDs solution was added into the solution and stirred for 2h. The suspension was centrifugated and washed using deionized water, dried at 80°C for 12h in a vacuum oven.

The as-prepared samples were investigated by X-ray diffractometer (D/Max-2500, Rigaku Corp) with Cu Kα radiation at 30kV and 30mV (λ=1.54056Å), which was operated in a 2θ range of 10–80° at a scanning speed of 0.02° per step. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of the samples were taken using Quanta 450. TEM images were taken on a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 with an acceleration voltage of 200kV. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas were calculated from Quantachrom Autosorb. The light absorption properties of solid photocatalysts were collected on UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrophotometer (DRS, JASCO, UV-550). The absorption spectra of liquid samples were taken on Tianmei Tech UV-1100. These EPR spectra were taken on a Bruker ECS106 X-Band spectrometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer at room temperature.

Investigation of photocatalytic performances of CQDs-TiO2 photocatalysts were conducted by degrading methylene blue (MB) under visible light irradiation. In a typical procedure, 50mg photocatalyst were put into 200mL of MB solution (10mg/L). This reaction vessel was located at 20cm away from the light source. Light source was 500W Xe lamp with a UV cutoff filter (λ>420nm). The solution was placed in dark for 30min to reach absorption and adsorption equilibrium. After that, the photocatalytic reaction was triggered when turning on the light. 1mL of suspension liquid was collected and filtered using 0.22µm filter at regular intervals. The MB solution was measured by a Tianmei Tech UV-1100.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and optical properties of water-soluble carbon QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon materials (graphene, carbon nanotube, carbon dots) are discovered to possess good stability, biocompatibility and conductivity. Therefore, they are often used as efficient components to modify others semiconductors due to the optoelectronic properties [18,29–32]. Owing to quantum size effect, nanoscaled CQDs exhibit a semiconductor-like electron transition in Fig.1 (a). The two broad peaks of absorption centered at 350 and 285nm are attributed to the n–π∗ transition of CO bond and the π–π∗ transition of CC bond, respectively [24,33,34]. Meanwhile, we observed that CO bond transition remains unchanged with increasing of reaction temperature. In contrast, CC bond transition shifted to short wavelength when reaction temperature was elevated, which maybe result from the condensation reaction among the l-ascorbic acid molecular. Simultaneously, the CC bond also shifted to short wavelength with the prolonging of reaction time in Fig.1(b).

Fluorescent properties of CQDs were investigated and shown in Fig.2 (a) from which we could observe excitation-dependent fluorescent spectra. The excitation-dependent fluorescent properties of CQDs demonstrated that the fluorescence results from irradiative recombination of excitons at the surface sites of CQDs where there are many different recombination paths. Significantly, the as-obtained CQDs are displayed to possess clear upconversion PL properties as shown in Fig.2(b). The upconversion PL properties of CDs can be attributed to the multiphoton active process, in which the simultaneous absorption of two or more photons leads to the emission at a shorter wavelength than the excitation wavelength (anti-Stokes type emission) [28]. Moreover, the upconversion process displays the ability of the as-prepared CQDs to absorb visible light even near IR spectrum.

The diameter of CQDs was measured by means of TEM and HRTEM, which displays that the highly monodispersed CQDs with about 5nm has been successfully fabricated in Fig.3 a. The surface organic groups of CQDs have been investigated through FTIR spectrum, as shown in Fig.3b. The peaks centered at 3460cm−1 and 1742cm−1, and 1180cm−1 are attributed to vibration of OH, CO, and CO, respectively, which demonstrates that polar and hydrophilic function groups such as carboxylics and hydroxyls were formed on the surface of CQDs because of condensation of l-ascorbic acid [20]. Therefore, these function groups allow CQDs to disperse well in the precursor solution of TiO2, accompany with the formation of TiO2, and anchor at the surface of TiO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of CQDs-TiO2 photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The above-mentioned photocatalysts have been synthesized via two-step process according to Scheme 1 . The solution including CQDs as a starting material was added into the precursor solution of TiO2, which could ensure CQDs to anchor on surface or interface of titania. Powder XRD patterns of the synthesized pure TiO2 and modified TiO2 with different amounts of CQDs solution are shown in Fig. 4 . The pure and modified TiO2 samples exhibit identical pattern with four main peaks at 25.4°, 38.1°, 47.7°, and 54.9°, which are attributed to (101), (004), (200) and (211) facets of anatase TiO2, respectively [35]. It clearly demonstrates that introduction of CQDs does not influence the crystal structure of TiO2. The average TiO2 crystallite size calculated by Scherer’s Formula is about 63.2nm, indicating such TiO2 powder is made up of nanocrystal particles.

To further investigate nanostructure of the samples, SEM images of pure TiO2 and CQDs-TiO2 were taken and displayed in Fig. 5 . These images show that the pure TiO2 sample consist of uniform microsphere and smaller particles with crystallite size ranging from several tens micrometers to one hundred nanometers. Although the change of TiO2 morphologies is negligible when CQDs added, the proportion of dispersed particles increased and the surface of TiO2 microsphere became rough, which suggests the modification would to some extent be beneficial for production of surface defect sites, thus improving photocatalytic performance.

The chemical nature of the interaction between the carbon dots and titania was investigated by measuring X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to explore whether new bond was formed between CQDs and titania, as present in Fig. 6 . The full survey spectrum in Fig.6(a) indicates the presence of carbon (C 1s), titanium (Ti 2p), and oxygen (O 1s) in the C QDs/TiO2 composites. In Fig.6(b), the two obvious peaks at 458.5 and 464.3eV are ascribed to the typical Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 of TiO2, respectively [36,37]. As displayed in Fig.6(c), the high resolution spectrum of C 1s was further deconvoluted into three peaks, where one peak at 284.6eV is attributed to the CC bond with a sp2 orbital and the other two peaks at 286.0 and 288.4eV are assigned to the CO and CO bonds, respectively, originating from the CQDs [38,39]. In Fig.6(d), the deconvoluted peaks of O 1s at 529.7, 530.4 and 532.7eV are ascribed to TiO, COH, and CO, respectively [40,41]. In addition, the XPS measurements suggest that no peak of the TiC bond is found in the CQDs/TiO2 composites, which indicates that the CQDs do not exist as a dopant in the composites [42], and rules out the formation of new bonds between the CQDs and TiO2. Therefore, the CQDs/TiO2 heterojunction composites were formed by closely electrostatic interaction between the CQDs and TiO2, which enables photoexcited charge carriers to transfer through the contact interface.

The photo-response ability of the prepared samples was measured by using UV–vis spectrum in Fig.7 (a). It could be observed that the absorption edge of CQDs-TiO2 has been obviously shifted toward visible light region, suggesting strongly absorption ability for sunlight. Meanwhile, the band gap of modified TiO2 calculated by means of Kubelka-Munk method [7] changes from 3.24eV to 3.09eV, as shown in Fig.7(b). These results indicate that the modification of CQDs can significantly enhance the ability of TiO2 to absorb visible light due to narrow band gap and upconversion of CQDs. This way of modification is different from the doping of carbon element where carbon atoms are inserted into the interface and surface lattice of TiO2 for forming new energy level in band gap of TiO2, leading to visible-light absorption[43,44]. Such modification could not only increase photo-absorption efficiency, but also strengthen photogenerated electron-hole pairs.

Furthermore, nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution were investigated to verify porous properties of the as-synthesized samples. It was found that the pristine TiO2 and CQDs-TiO2 samples exhibit the similar type-IV isotherm, which is characteristic isotherm curve of mesoporous materials, as shown in Fig.8 (a). It is well known that the mesoporous properties mainly stem from aggregation of nanoparticles. In addition, the calculated BET surface area of CQDs-TiO2 (205m2/g) is larger than that of pure TiO2 (192m2/g) because of the introduction of CQDs. When coupling CQDs with TiO2 microspheres, the CQDs anchored on the surface of TiO2 microspheres and increased the surface roughness of TiO2 microspheres, resulting in the formation of interface between CQDs and TiO2, which could provide more active sites for photocatalytic reaction. Furthermore, pore size distributions are 2.4nm for CQDs-TiO2 and 2.9nm for TiO2, as displayed in Fig.8(b), which testifies that these samples possess mesoporous properties. Such mesoporous architecture is beneficial for catalytic reaction because it can provide effective paths for movement of reactants and products.

To evaluate photocatalytic performance of CQDs-TiO2 samples under visible light irradiation, methylene blue (MB) was used as target pollutant. As shown in Fig.9 (a), the degradation efficiency of CQDs-TiO2 over MB is significantly higher than that of the controlled pure TiO2. When the volume of CQDs used was 10mL, degradation efficiency is highest, up to 90%, which is 3.6 times as high as that of pure TiO2. It is interesting that, with the increase of CQDs addition from 5mL to 10mL, the catalytic activity of the CQDs-TiO2 increased rapidly due to enhanced absorbance for visible light and improved separation efficiency of photo-generated charge carriers. Nevertheless, further increase of CQDs content to 15mL leads to apparent decrease of photocatalytic performance. Owing to small size of QDs suspending in the solution, the amount of QDs would influence distribution of QDs on the surface of metal oxide. In comparison with low usage of QDs, when excess QDs are used, the excess QDs would form a continuous film which covers upon the surface of metal oxide supporter and hinders light harvesting and charge transfer [45]. Therefore, we hypothesize that excess CQDs induce aggregation of CQDs on the surface of TiO2, which causes the decreasing of the separation efficiency of photo-induced electron-hole pairs [46]. The kinetic rates, k of CQDs-TiO2 for degradation of MB under visible light illumination are presented in Fig.9(b) and Fig. S1. It is observed that the introduction of CQDs significantly prompts reaction kinetic process where 10mL CQDs-TiO2 achieves rate constant of degradation 6.5 times higher than pure TiO2.

It is well-known that pH value of reaction solution has a non-negligible impact on degradation rate owing to the relationship between surface potential of nanostructured catalysis and pH value in aqueous solution [47]. For our catalytic system, existence of charged functional groups on the surface of CQDs-TiO2, it is necessary to investigate effect of pH value on degradation process. When varying pH value of solution including MB and catalysts, the degradation efficiency and kinetic rate fluctuated, as shown in Figs. 10 and S2. Notably, the catalytic activity is higher under alkaline condition. It is probable that the acidic oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CQDs plays crucial role in the recombination of photo-induced electrons and holes during the degradation reaction process [24,48]. Under base environment, these functional groups become more electronegativity, which is beneficial for electron transferring between CQDs and TiO2. The EPR is used to capture the reactive oxygen species (ROS) produced in the photocatalysis process. The recorded data of the CQDs-TiO2 system are displayed in Fig. 11 . We could apparently observe the strongly characteristic peaks of hydroxyl radicals (
OH), which indicates that the photoinduced holes under visible light irradiation could react with absorbed H2O and produce hydroxyl radicals [49,50]. Then, the hydroxyl radical oxides and damages the organic pollutants anchored at the surface of the catalyst.

On the base of the above results, we elucidate that the enhanced photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation could be attributed to the formation of heterojunction between CQDs and TiO2. Owing to the quantum confinement effect and surface oxygen-containing functional groups, it is found that the CQDs possess unique upconversion property. This property could make CQDs absorb low energetic photons and release high energetic photons. When coupled CQDs with TiO2, the nanosized CQDs can anchor on the surface of TiO2, where two energy transfer processes happen between CQDs and TiO2. When the CQDs-TiO2 composite is irradiated by visible light, the CQDs are excited by high energetic photons and produce electrons and holes, where the photoinduced electrons transfer from the excited state to conduction band of TiO2 and mainly contribute to production of superoxide radicals. The holes stay at the ground states and produced hydroxyl radicals, as the depicted (step I) in Fig. 12 . Simultaneously, the rest of lower energetic photons in the visible light are absorbed by the CQDs to produce electron-hole pairs that are to recombine at ground state of CQDs to emit higher energy photons because of the upconversion properties of CQDs. After that, higher energy photons could excite TiO2 host and produce electron-hole pairs for photocatalytic reaction, as displayed (step II) in Fig. 12. Therefore, the upconversion process could further increase utilization efficiency of solar energy and thus widen photo-response range. Moreover, the oxygen-containing groups to some degree control the transferring efficiency of electrons between CQDs and TiO2 due to variation of functional group electronegativity with the reaction conditions, which could influence the yield of hydroxyl and superoxide radicals.

In summary, this work focused on improving efficient utilization of Ti-based photocatylyst for sunlight by taking advantage of upconversion properties of water soluble carbon QDs. We find that CQDs synthesized based on hydrothermal method is verified to possess obvious upconversion properties that could transfer low energy photons to high energy photons. Therefore, introduction of CQDs plays crucial role in widening light-response range and increasing quantum yield. CQDs-TiO2 displays enhanced photocatalytic performance that is 3.6 times as high as pure TiO2 under visible light irradiation. Based on analysis of the results, we proposed a reasonable model to explain the photocatalytic procedure under visible light irradiation. These results reveal that carbon QDs, being a member of carbon materials, provides a new way to design excellent and nontoxic catalysts.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported financially by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (Nos. NSFC21207015, 21507029, 21501138), the Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province, China (No. 2015CFB177), and the Key Laboratory of Industrial Ecology and Environmental Engineering, China Ministry of Education.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2017.01.121.
